turns.user_message: Hi, thanks for seeing me. Yeah, I've been smoking for about 10 years now. I've tried to quit a few times before, but it never seems to stick. I'm worried that quitting will be really hard and disrupt my daily routine. I smoke during my lunch break, after work with colleagues... it's just a big part of my social life, you know?

past_strategies.strategy: tried to quit a few times before

step2_rag_chunks: Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.
Support from friends, family, or healthcare professionals can improve the chances of successfully quitting smoking.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

relevant_strategies.strategy: tried to quit a few times before

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the MOTIVATION phase. You understand their triggers. Now explore their motivation: why do they want to quit — is it health, family, cost, doctor advice? Have they tried before and what happened? Be empathetic. Do NOT push strategies yet.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.
STYLE RULES — follow these strictly:
- Keep your response to 1-3 sentences maximum
- Ask only ONE question per response
- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal
[Session Phase: 3 — Motivation]

step7_response: I can sense how hard it must be for you to consider quitting smoking, especially when it's such a big part of your social life. turns.user_message: Well, like I said, I smoke during my lunch break, and it's just something I've always done. I'm worried that if I quit, I'll have to find something else to do during that time, and it'll be awkward. And after work, we always go out for drinks and smoke together... I don't want to be the only one not smoking. I'm worried I'll feel left out or like I'm missing out on fun.

Therapist: I totally understand, Rachel. It sounds like you're concerned about how quitting will affect your daily habits and social life. Can you tell me more about what you mean by "disrupt my daily routine"? What specific things are you worried about changing?

Therapist:
step7_response: So you're worried about feeling left out or missing out on fun with your colleagues when you quit. Can you tell me more about what you value in your relationships with them - is it the socializing, the sense of belonging, or something else?

turns.user_message: This time, I feel like I'm more ready. I've been thinking about quitting for a while now, and I've been trying to prepare myself mentally. I've also been talking to some friends who have quit, and they've been really supportive. And, to be honest, my doctor told me that my blood pressure is getting a bit high, and I don't want to end up with more serious health problems. So, I think I'm just more motivated this time around.
step2_rag_chunks: Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.
Many people find that writing down their reasons for quitting smoking helps maintain motivation during difficult moments.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

[Recent Conversation]

Patient: Hi, thanks for seeing me. Yeah, I've been smoking for about 10 years now. I've tried to quit a few times before, but it never seems to stick. I'm worried that quitting will be really hard and disrupt my daily routine. I smoke during my lunch break, after work with colleagues... it's just a big part of my social life, you know?

Therapist: I totally understand, Rachel. It sounds like you're concerned about how quitting will affect your daily habits and social life. Can you tell me more about what you mean by "disrupt my daily routine"? What specific things are you worried about changing?

Patient: Well, like I said, I smoke during my lunch break, and it's just something I've always done. I'm worried that if I quit, I'll have to find something else to do during that time, and it'll be awkward. And after work, we always go out for drinks and smoke together... I don't want to be the only one not smoking. I'm worried I'll feel left out or like I'm missing out on fun.

Therapist: I get it. It sounds like you're worried about losing a sense of connection with your colleagues and friends. That's a really valid concern. But I want to ask you, what do you think would be the benefits of quitting smoking, despite these challenges?

Patient: Hmm... well, I guess I wouldn't have to worry about the health risks anymore, and I wouldn't have to spend so much money on cigarettes. And I've heard that quitting can actually improve your energy levels and overall mood... which would be great for my job and just life in general. But, I'm still worried about gaining weight and experiencing withdrawal symptoms... I've heard those can be really tough.

Patient: This time, I feel like I'm more ready. I've been thinking about quitting for a while now, and I've been trying to prepare myself mentally. I've also been talking to some friends who have quit, and they've been really supportive. And, to be honest, my doctor told me that my blood pressure is getting a bit high, and I don't want to end up with more serious health problems. So, I think I'm just more motivated this time around.
Current patient message:

This time, I feel like I'm more ready. I've been thinking about quitting for a while now, and I've been trying to prepare myself mentally. I've also been talking to some friends who have quit, and they've been really supportive. And, to be honest, my doctor told me that my blood pressure is getting a bit high, and I don't want to end up with more serious health problems. So, I think I'm just more motivated this time around.
[REASONING]
1. Phase I am in: Planning
2. What does the phase instruction tell me to focus on?

[RESPONSE]
Therapist:
step7_response: I notice you've been preparing yourself mentally and getting support from friends who have quit. That takes a lot of courage. One thing that might help you stay motivated is to write down your reasons for quitting and put them somewhere visible, like on your fridge or mirror. Would you be willing to try that?
